routes.name: Alpental Ski Area Summer/Spring Route

trailhead.name: Alpental Base Lodge
trailhead.parking: Alpental parking lot; verify access post-road-closure

routes.descent: Edelweiss Bowl scree run or reverse approach

routes.name: East Ridge (Degenhardt Route)
routes.first_ascent: Unknown; named after peak first ascensionist William Degenhardt

pitch_breakdown.terrain: Mixed class 4–5, traverse to south face
South face traverse and wrap-around to final pitches

rock_quality.hazards: Significant loose rock and rockfall risk throughout

protection.density: Minimal on most pitches except first; single rack of nuts sufficient on upper pitches
protection.strategy: Place protection on first pitch; minimal pro on subsequent pitches (choss-fest)

gear.runners: Bring plenty of double runners (5+) and slings for choss
gear.protection: Nuts, small–medium cams preferred over larger gear
gear.safety: Helmet (required), rock shoes or approach shoes

approach_to_route.terrain: Steep descent into Crescent Creek Basin (mixed snow/talus, 400 ft wet slabby 3rd/4th class climbing), ~1-hour rising traverse across basin
approach_to_route.route_finding: Marked by cairns but can be tricky; exposed terrain

time_estimate.total_from_camp: 8–10 hours round-trip (depending on conditions and team speed)
time_estimate.notes: High variability due to loose rock and routefinding challenges

hazards: Extremely loose rock and rockfall (primary hazard)
Crumbly rock quality reduces security
Exposure on ridge; significant air
Weather exposure; weather windows critical
Routefinding challenges in upper terrain
Wet slabby terrain on approach (3rd/4th class)--slipping risk
routes.weather_exposure: Very exposed; afternoon thunderstorm risk; high altitude weather changes rapidly

descent.anchor_type: Downclimbing, some rappel anchors if needed
descent.total_time: Included in 8–10 hour estimate from camp

routes.user_notes: Route is less popular due to choss factor and loose rock hazard. Rock quality is arguably worse than reputation suggests. Expect to manage protection carefully and avoid dislodging large amounts of debris on partners below.

routes.name: North Face / North Buttress (Stoddard Route)
routes.first_ascent_original: 1961 by Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, David Hiser, Mike Swayne (III 5.7, line of least resistance to right of crest)
routes.first_ascent_stoddard: 1984 by John Stoddard (bold solo onsight ascent of cleaner, knife-edge ridge crest; 5.8+)
routes.popularity: Classic, committing line; fewer ascents than West Ridge due to difficulty and commitment
routes.route_description: Impressive north side rises dramatically 2,500 feet from McMillan Cirque to summit. Route ascends directly from base to summit via prominent buttress.

pitch_breakdown.protection: Protection possible but not always necessary

pitch_breakdown.terrain: Low-angle 5th class rock (can be simul-climbed as single mega-pitch or broken into pitches)
pitch_breakdown.protection: Moderate pro placement; some runners useful

pitch_breakdown.section: Upper buttress (2,000+ ft to summit block)
pitch_breakdown.terrain: Knife-edge ridge of blocky rock (Stoddard route); steeper, more technical
pitch_breakdown.protection: Crux pitches require good protection; blocky rock quality variable

rock_quality.mid_sections: Generally solid on crest; varied off-crest
rock_quality.upper_sections: Clean, solid rock on main crest (Stoddard line)
rock_quality.summit_approach: Slightly looser blocky rock near false summit and west peak
rock_quality.overall_assessment: Much better than East Ridge; Stoddard line features inspiring, cleaner rock

protection.type: Trad (cams, nuts) and natural anchors (slings, rappel stations)
protection.strategy: Medium alpine rack emphasized; cams preferred to nuts on upper pitches
protection.lower_sections: Can simul-climb with minimal pro; protection available if desired
protection.upper_pitches: Better protection; crux sections require good placements

gear.rack: Medium alpine rack with emphasis on medium cams and slings
gear.runners: Multiple slings and alpine runners for belay anchors
gear.ice_protection: Ice axe and crampons necessary for glacier traversal and snow
gear.ice_screws: 1–2 ice screws for glacier moat protection if needed
gear.belay: Sling and bolt anchors on upper route; rappel stations marked

approach_to_route.from_crescent_creek_basin: 2–3 hours even for fast parties to base of route at McMillan Cirque
approach_to_route.terrain: Traverse Crescent Creek Basin (scree/talus with hidden snow and ice ramps); ascend to col between Himmelhorn and Ottohorn
approach_to_route.glacier_crossing: Descent from col via downclimbing steep snow/ice or rappelling to McMillan Cirque; traverse across glaciated terrain to base of North Buttress
approach_to_route.moat: Negotiate glacier moat to reach climbing base; ice screw may be helpful

time_estimate.climb_only: 5–7 hours (rough estimate; can be simul-climbed in sections to save time)
time_estimate.descent: Not separately documented; typically 2–3 hours via West Ridge descent
time_estimate.total_trip: 3–4 days total (includes 1–2 day approach, climb day, exit day)

hazards: Objective hazard: rockfall and ice from above (2,500 feet of elevation; exposure to entire north side)
Loose blocky rock in upper sections (though better than East Ridge)
Long, exposed climbing (committing); weather window critical
Glacier hazards: moats, crevasses (may be belayed), avalanche exposure (generally low risk in summer)
Complex routefinding in upper sections (especially in poor visibility)
Exposure to afternoon weather changes at altitude
routes.weather_exposure: Highly exposed; weather windows critical for safety

descent.method: West Ridge descent (easy and uneventful on ridge)
descent.hazards: Loose rock on off-route sections; significant off-route bushwhacking on return adds ~6 hours
descent.anchor_type: Natural anchors and slings; no fixed rappels required if staying on ridge
descent.notes: Return bushwhacking to base camp is time-consuming; allow extra hours for route-finding exit

routes.climbing_style_notes: Original 1961 route follows line of least resistance (easier angle, right of crest). Stoddard 1984 route follows cleaner, more aesthetic knife-edge crest (higher grade, better rock, more inspiring but more exposed).

first_ascent.significance: Took 3 years for second ascent (2010); very few ascents since

approach.terrain_type: PCT trail, then off-trail bushwhacking through alder and forest
approach.key_crossings: North Fork River log crossing, Grizzly Creek, extensive alder tunnel

route_description.climbing_method: Mostly simul-climbing on 3rd-4th class, belayed sections on harder terrain
route_description.rock_quality: Distinctly alpine throughout; excellent gneiss on harder pitches
route_description.crux_sections: Two pitches of 5.10 crack climbing on southeast peak headwall; 5.10 downclimb on overhanging pitch with 5,000 ft exposure below
route_description.notable_features: Dramatic ridge with exceptional sustained exposure; glacier-to-rock transition crossing; highly technical for alpine climb

gear.bivy_requirements: Summit bivy acceptable (tight for 2-3 people); sleeping bag rated to 30-40°F minimum

steps: Two 30m rappels to soft snow field
Skier's left exit; scramble crumbly ledges
Descend obvious snow couloir
Looker's right to avoid waterfall cliffs
5,000 ft cumulative descent; poor rock quality on exit

primary: Extreme exposure (5,000 ft sections)
Brittle rock - high rockfall risk especially on descent
Overhanging 5.10 downclimb pitch with massive exposure
Poor rock quality on rappel anchors
Difficult glacier-to-buttress moat crossing
Weather: afternoon thunderstorms, temperature swings
secondary: Remoteness: 3-year gap between first and second ascent
Endurance: 12+ hour climbing day after 11-12 hour approach

routes.ascent_count: Rare; only ~2-3 ascents beyond first ascent (as of 2021)

first_ascent.significance: Classic alpine route; most popular on the mountain

approach.terrain_type: PCT trail south, North Fork Bridge Creek trail northwest, then bushwhacking
creek_crossings: Multiple water crossings in forest section
approach.terrain_obstacles: Alder bushwhacking (1-2 hours of dense growth), increasing overgrowth on North Fork trail, Class 3 slabs right of waterfall

vertical_sections: Rope up for glacier travel (crampons required)
Two pitches low 5th class on red ledges
route_description.rock_quality: Poor to fair - exposed granitic slabs on lower section, bomber sections on crest
route_description.crux_pitches: Glacier-to-buttress transition via collapsed snow moat (Class 4 ledge traversal); left arrete (5.5 moves)

gear.bivy_requirements: Summit bivy (space for ~3 people); lightweight pad preferred; sleeping bag rated to 34°F minimum
gear.water: No reliable source on route - carry sufficient supply

descent.method: Rappels and scrambling via South Face
steps: 2 rappels to Black Tooth Notch (traverse-y, 30m pitches)
Class 4 ledge scramble to cairn (may require up/down scrambling)
4 additional rappels into southwest couloir (with loose rock hazard)
Class 2-3 terrain on gully descent
Open meadow and burn zone descent to Park Creek trail
descent.alternative_descent: South gully approach; avoid waterfall cliffs via looker's right

primary: Moat crossing: Variable difficulty, potentially impassable late season
Abundant loose rock: Rockfall hazard on rappels
Glacier crevasses
Overhead hazard: Seracs and icefall from receding glacier above
Extreme heat on exit in late summer (98°F+ in Stehekin area)
secondary: Poor rock quality requiring careful anchor placement
Limited bail-out options (loose terrain, no rap stations)

routes.popularity: Most popular route on mountain; on many climbers' wish lists

first_ascent.climbers: Wolf Bauer, Philip Dickett, Joe Halwax, Jack Hossack, George MacGowan
first_ascent.significance: Original route; still the least technical

options.name: Bridge Creek Approach (most common)
options.terrain_type: PCT trail, North Fork Bridge Creek trail, then off-trail to glacier
options.key_landmarks: Open area at base of Goode at trail end

options.name: Park Creek Approach (Stehekin)
options.notes: Road washouts above Highbridge make this approach less attractive

route_description.glacier_approach: Traverse west end of glacier to reach couloir
route_description.notable_features: Notch requiring downclimbing onto small glacier; steep gully with loose rock
route_description.crux_section: 300-foot headwall requiring testing each hold, intensified by ice pockets
route_description.rock_quality: Largely poor to terrible; loose rock throughout
route_description.snow_ice_conditions: Temperature-dependent; can have thin ice pockets; snow patches often last into August

specifics: Rock (minimal - for rappel anchors if needed)
Rope (30-60m, for safety anchor or rappel)
gear.rope_usage: Recommended for crevasse rescue on glacier approach

steps: Descend obvious snow couloir (direct route)
Class 3-4 scrambling in gully
Trail descent to base camp
descent.technical_difficulty: Low for descent; loose rock is primary hazard

primary: Loose and poor quality rock: Major rockfall hazard throughout
Glacier instability: Rumbling reported; seracs and crevasses
Icy conditions: Temperature-dependent thin ice on rock
Steep gully exposure
secondary: Moat crossing: Variable difficulty depending on melt stage
Weather: Afternoon thunderstorms, wind exposure at summit area

routes.best_season: Late July to August (snow patches stable into August)
routes.comparative_difficulty: Easiest technical grade, but poor rock quality makes it serious; rockfall hazard exceeds grade rating

routes.name: Fisher Chimneys
routes.area_path: North America > USA > Washington > North Cascades > Mount Shuksan Massif
routes.description: Tortuous chimney system weaving through Shuksan Arm, crossing White Salmon Glacier, steep snow slope, Upper Curtis Glacier traverse, second steep step on Sulphide Glacier, finishing 3/4-class summit pyramid. Second-most climbed route on mountain.

watch_out.description: 3/4-class summit block has fair amount of loose rock; allow extra time to descend if other parties present; expect rockfall from above when congested
watch_out.terrain: Summit pyramid (final 100 vertical feet)
watch_out.timing: Year-round, worst during descent with traffic
watch_out.frequency: Common when parties queue on summit

watch_out.description: Northwest corner melts to bare hard glacier ice; requires crampons (aluminum adequate) but conditions tenuous; ice climbing tools may be needed
watch_out.terrain: Upper Curtis Glacier traverse, ~2000 ft elevation band

watch_out.description: Debris fields on both sides of valley indicate consistent spring/early summer avalanche activity from surrounding slopes; rain can soften/weight snow dramatically increasing instability
watch_out.terrain: Approach gully and surrounding slopes to Shuksan Arm
watch_out.frequency: Multiple slides per spring/early summer season

watch_out.description: Tortuous route with multiple gully options; wrong gully has moss/lichen-covered rock; correct route marked by trail and clean rock from boot traffic; easy to lose route in early season snow
watch_out.terrain: Shuksan Arm, first 3000 vertical feet
watch_out.frequency: Navigation errors possible for first-timers or in poor visibility

watch_out.hazard: Crevasse/bergschrund exposure on White Salmon Glacier
watch_out.description: Temporary crevasse bridges deteriorate late season; bergschrunds open up; in early season heavy snowpack bridges may be solid
watch_out.terrain: White Salmon Glacier section, ~1000 vertical feet

routes.name: North Face (Crystal Glacier Route)
routes.area_path: North America > USA > Washington > North Cascades > Mount Shuksan Massif
routes.description: Ascends snow/glacier shoulder dividing White Salmon cirque from Price Glacier cirque. Climbs 40-50 degree snow and ice, may steepen to 50 degrees just before topping out onto shoulder between Crystal and Hanging Glacier. Can descend via White Salmon Glacier or reverse route.

watch_out.hazard: Active Hanging Glacier avalanches (very large volumes)
watch_out.description: Hanging Glacier above White Salmon Creek area is very active and produces bus-sized to house-sized avalanches capable of sweeping entire cirque; debris reaches base of climbing area
watch_out.terrain: Entire North Face cirque, White Salmon Creek drainage
watch_out.frequency: Multiple major slides per week in peak season

watch_out.hazard: Serac calving over snow chutes (Crystal Glacier)
watch_out.description: Overhanging seracs hang above snow chutes feeding into climbing area; bus-sized ice blocks have calved directly above climbers; ice falls into crevasses below
watch_out.terrain: Serac line above upper snow chutes
watch_out.frequency: Rare but documented (has occurred during climbs)

watch_out.hazard: Steep snow slope avalanche exposure (40-50 degrees)
watch_out.description: Main climbing terrain is sustained 40-50 degree snow and ice; steep enough for sloughing; can transition to steeper sections near shoulder; rain or solar gain can trigger slides
watch_out.terrain: Full line from base to shoulder, 2500 vertical feet
watch_out.timing: Year-round, worst post-rain or high-sun days
watch_out.frequency: Slough hazard daily in warm conditions

watch_out.description: Route faces north-facing aspect but gets significant afternoon sun on east side; high alpine weather exposure at 9000+ ft; prone to rapid weather changes; strong winds possible
watch_out.terrain: Upper route above 8500 ft elevation

watch_out.description: Crevasses present throughout approach and upper glacier; can be well-bridged in early season, open and extensive by late season
watch_out.terrain: Crystal Glacier and approach glaciers

routes.name: Price Glacier
routes.area_path: North America > USA > Washington > North Cascades > Mount Shuksan Massif
routes.description: Famous Grade IV alpine ice route from Fifty Classic Climbs of North America. Steep snow and alpine ice on Price Glacier; technically one of the hardest alpine ice routes in contiguous US after first ascent. Approach via Price Lake. 2-3 day expedition; descent typically via easier routes (Sulphide or White Salmon Glacier).

watch_out.hazard: Massive overhanging seracs with ice calving
watch_out.description: Bus-sized piece of ice calved off overhanging serac directly above climber; TV-sized rock/ice came down gully between Nooksack Tower and main Price Glacier lobe; seracs impend over upper climbing terrain
watch_out.terrain: Upper Price Glacier, serac band around 8500-8800 ft

watch_out.description: Giant crevasses and overhanging bergschrunds; route determined by crevasse/bergschrund positions; early season: heavy snowpack solidly bridges them (simple ascent); late season: extensive bare ice, forced to drop into and climb out of overhanging crevasse/bergschrund sides
watch_out.terrain: Entire glacier, main hazard zone 7500-8500 ft elevation
watch_out.frequency: Year-round; conditions vary dramatically by season phase

watch_out.hazard: Late-season ice climbing difficulty and exposure
watch_out.description: In late season (August-September) glacier is much icier; some routes require ice tools and ice screws; 50+ degree ice possible; mixed alpine terrain; technically demanding (AI2-3 grade confirms difficulty)
watch_out.terrain: Upper glacier, steeper pitches
watch_out.frequency: All late-season ascents encounter these conditions

watch_out.hazard: Rockfall from Nooksack Tower and surrounding peaks
watch_out.description: TV-sized rock documented falling down gully between Nooksack Tower and main Price Glacier; loose rock common in couloirs and side drainage; afternoon sun triggers more rockfall
watch_out.terrain: Gullies flanking glacier, surrounding ridges

watch_out.description: Most competent alpinists need 2-3 days; descent typically via easier routes (Sulphide or White Salmon Glacier), not via ascent; adds navigation complexity and route-planning demands
watch_out.terrain: Descent route differs from ascent; must know both routes

routes.name: South Spur (South Climb)

approach.access_road: Forest Road 80 (South Climb), steep and narrow with sharp switchbacks, high-clearance vehicle recommended
approach.permits: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass required May 1–September 30 (mandatory above 7,000 ft); free self-issued wilderness permit off-season; $5 day-use parking fee or annual pass
approach.seasonal_notes: Best May-September; snow coverage decreases through season (significant June-July, sparse below 7,500 ft by August); June: bring ice ax/crampons to Lunch Counter; July: crevasse navigation tricky; August-September: dry, loose rock/scree dominant
approach.parking: Cold Springs Camp has two main parking areas that fill quickly; park on roadside if needed; RVs/trailers prohibited

route_beta.crux_description: Moderately steep 30° snow slopes from Lunch Counter (~9,250 ft) to false summit (11,657 ft), then final 200-yard traverse to true summit
route_beta.snow_ice_conditions: June: compacted summer snow, icy crust, crampons/ice ax essential; July: sun-cupped ice crust, possible melt streams; August: mostly dry, loose volcanic scree; September: unpredictable, rapid weather changes, possible rime ice
route_beta.technical_details: Non-technical snow scramble; ascend northeast up main snowfield on south spur, directly to false summit, then north-northwest to south side of summit formation, then 30° snow to broad summit area

hazards: Loose volcanic rock and rockfall, especially when melting exposes rock
Sudden weather changes (100°F to -48°F documented in storms)
Glissading hazards—crevasse-free on south side but obstacles may hide in chutes
Sun-cupped snow creating rolling turns on descent
Thermal features/altered rock on summit plateau—unstable altered rock can spawn debris avalanches
High altitude exposure and potential AMS
gear_required: Ice axe (essential June-July, optional late summer)
Crampons (essential June-July, microspikes acceptable late summer)

time_estimate.note: Commonly done in 2 days with overnight at Lunch Counter; can be done as vigorous one-day ascent (10-12 hours)

routes.descent: Same route in reverse; glissading possible early season on upper slopes; descent similar time to ascent
sources: WTA Mount Adams South Climb guide
Alpine Ascents International Mount Adams guide
Gifford Pinchot National Forest Mt. Adams Climbing Conditions Report (July 2026)
Mountain Project - South Spur route database

routes.name: North Ridge (North Cleaver)

approach.access_road: Forest Road 23 to Forest Road 2329 (Takhlakh Lake), rough gravel road; high-clearance vehicle required
approach.permits: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass required June 1–September 30 (mandatory above 7,000 ft); wilderness permit required; $15 weekends, $10 mid-week ($5 day-use if parking)
approach.seasonal_notes: Best in June-July when snow is significant; becomes loose rock scramble by August-September; second most popular route but much more remote feeling than South Spur
approach.parking: Killen Creek gravel parking area, capacity 8 vehicles, 24-hour access; very limited

route_beta.crux_description: Between Adams and Lava Glaciers; steepest sections around 40°; primarily loose volcanic scree scramble with mixed snow/rock depending on season
route_beta.snow_ice_conditions: June-July: steep snow climbing, 40° sections; August+: mostly loose rock; very loose gravel and scrambling, no technical climbing but exposed terrain
route_beta.technical_details: Route-finding: traverse from Killen Creek trail past PCT to high camp at 6,900 ft; ascend between Adams and Lava Glaciers to summit; class 2-3 scrambling with very loose rock throughout

hazards: Very loose volcanic rock and scree—constant rockfall danger to parties below
Loose gravel on steep terrain—exposure hazard
Many parties go through considerable trouble to avoid this route due to loose rock
Crevasse fields on adjacent glaciers if route deviates
Weather exposure at higher elevation
Long approach with few escape routes
gear_required: Climbing boots with good ankle support
Pickets and screws if establishing belays

time_estimate.note: Typically done in 3 days; day 1 approach to basecamp, day 2 summit push, day 3 descent

routes.descent: North Ridge descent line is notorious for loose rock causing rockfall; often parties descend via Adams Glacier when possible; very loose scree all the way down
sources: Northwest Mountain School Mt. Adams guides
SummitPost North Ridge - North Cleaver route database
Mountain Project North Ridge climbing database
The Mountaineers Mount Adams ice climbing routes guide
Eric's Base Camp Mount Adams North Ridge trip reports

routes.name: Adams Glacier

route_start.note: Adams Glacier base, approximately 1-2 hours from high camp

approach.access_road: Forest Road 23 to Forest Road 2329 (Takhlakh Lake), 5.7 miles from Taklakh Lake at 4,584 ft
approach.permits: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass required June 1–September 30; wilderness permit required; $15 weekends, $10 mid-week
approach.seasonal_notes: Generally a window early each summer (late May/June) when route in good condition; early season provides best conditions; crevasse negotiation tricky July onward; route-finding very difficult mid-season
approach.parking: Killen Creek trailhead, 8-vehicle capacity, rough gravel

route_beta.crux_description: Tortured 4,000-foot icefall between two rock ridges; requires diligent route-finding; steep snow climbing and several pitches of alpine ice; most ascend right side about two-thirds way, then work left to avoid crevasses
route_beta.snow_ice_conditions: June: compacted summer snow, moderate-to-steep sections 45-50°, some alpine ice; July: sun-softened conditions increase icefall danger; August+: exposed rock, dry conditions, difficult route-finding
route_beta.technical_details: Approach: Killen Creek trail past PCT to high camp at 6,900 ft in meadows at glacial lake (7,650 ft) or established high camp (7,000 ft); route ascends more than 5,000 vertical feet; most sections allow simul-soloing with picket and running belay protection; 2+ sections require ice screws and belay anchors, including crevasse crossings; upper icefall sections require careful navigation

hazards: Icefall danger—must start very early to be off glacier before sun heating intensifies
Crevasse fields in mid-glacier section, especially mid-to-late season
Variable route conditions year-to-year—route-finding essential
Upper icefall sections with falling ice/rock danger
Heavily crevassed eastern side of summit ice cap
Very loose volcanic rock on North Ridge descent alternative
gear_required: Ice screws (2-3 for protection on steeper sections)

time_estimate.note: Typically 3-day trip; day 1 approach, day 2 summit push (start pre-dawn), day 3 descent

routes.descent: Option 1: descend Adams Glacier; Option 2: traverse to North Ridge for descent (exposes to loose rock hazard); predawn approach and descent timing critical to avoid icefall
sources: Mountain Project Adams Glacier route database
The Mountaineers Adams Glacier climbing trip reports
Ice and Trail Mt. Adams Adams Glacier guide
Eric's Base Camp Adams Glacier trip reports

routes.name: North Lyman Glacier

route_start.note: North Lyman Glacier, approximately 1 hour from high camp

approach.access_road: Forest Road 23 to Forest Road 2329, same as Adams Glacier route
approach.permits: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass required June 1–September 30; wilderness permit required
approach.seasonal_notes: Primarily late spring route; characterized by deep crevasses and icefalls cascading from summit ice cap; descends from 11,400 ft to 7,800 ft; heavily crevassed terrain increases route-finding difficulty mid-to-late season

route_beta.crux_description: Glacier characterized by deep crevasses and many icefalls cascading down between two rock ridges; requires ice climbing skills; mostly moderate and steep snow with alpine ice sections; traverse from camp takes approximately 1 hour
route_beta.snow_ice_conditions: Late spring: moderate and steep snow, alpine ice sections; midsummer: heavily crevassed with difficult route-finding; sun-hardened ice common in early season
route_beta.technical_details: Shares approach with Adams Glacier and North Face of Northwest Ridge routes; high camp in meadows at 6,900 ft; traverse to Lyman takes 1 hour; ice climbing on mixed terrain with ice screws and belays required on steeper sections; negotiating crevasses and route-finding becomes tricky to impossible by mid-July

hazards: Deep crevasses throughout glacier—high crevasse danger
Icefalls cascading from summit ice cap
Heavily crevassed eastern side creates navigational difficulty
Icefall and rockfall danger on upper sections
Weather exposure, rapid changes possible
Route-finding extremely difficult mid-to-late season
gear_required: Ice screws (3-4 for protection and anchors)

time_estimate.note: 3-day trip recommended; extensive route-finding and crevasse navigation require careful pacing

routes.descent: Typically reverse route on Lyman Glacier or traverse to alternate descent
sources: The Mountaineers North Lyman Glacier climbing guide

routes.name: North Face of Northwest Ridge

route_start.note: North Face of Northwest Ridge, accessible from Adams Glacier area

approach.access_road: Forest Road 23 to Forest Road 2329, same as Adams Glacier/North Lyman approaches
approach.permits: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass required June 1–September 30; wilderness permit required
approach.seasonal_notes: Early season (June-July) optimal; large bergschrund usually prevents access at base, requiring ascent via Adams Glacier before traversing to route; rotten rock and mixed conditions common

route_beta.crux_description: Route runs between Adams and loose Northwest ridge; ascends at 40-45° good neve in upper sections; typically not accessible at base due to large bergschrund; requires crossing crevasses on west side of Adams Glacier before reaching North Face terrain; climbers left of northwest ridge with typical rotten rock on snow, ice, and snice; good neve above 10,000 ft
route_beta.snow_ice_conditions: June-July: snow and ice dominant, 40-45° sections of good neve; typically blocked at base by large bergschrund; rotten rock common mid-face; August+: significant rockfall danger as ice melts
route_beta.technical_details: Access via Adams Glacier; stay climber's left of northwest ridge; negotiate crevasses on west side of Adams Glacier to reach North Face terrain; running belays with pickets or screws on steeper sections; ascend to summit via upper snowfield

hazards: Large bergschrund at base prevents direct access—requires glacier crossing
Crevasses on west side of Adams Glacier
Rotten rock on mixed terrain—loose rock hazard
Steep terrain (40-45°) with exposure
Rockfall danger as season progresses and ice melts
Weather changes rapid at elevation

time_estimate.note: 3-day trip; requires careful route-finding and early-season timing

routes.descent: Typically traverse back to Adams Glacier or North Ridge descent; loose rock hazard on descent
sources: Mountain Project North Face of NW Ridge route database
The Mountaineers Mount Adams ice climbing routes guide

routes.name: Wilson Glacier Headwall (East Face)

wilson_glacier.note: Wilson Glacier terminus, source of Little Muddy Creek

approach.access_road: Bird Creek Road from south/southeast approach (alternate from Killen Creek/north routes); requires high-clearance vehicle
approach.permits: Mt. Adams Climbing Pass required June 1–September 30; wilderness permit required
approach.seasonal_notes: Early season only (May-June) when headwall is in ice condition; extremely dangerous mid-to-late season due to rockfall and warm ice conditions; route generally unrepeated—very few ascents documented
approach.parking: Limited parking on Bird Creek Road approach

route_beta.crux_description: Steep headwall climbing on mixed rock and ice; Wilson Glacier descends from ~10,600 ft to 7,400 ft terminus; headwall route climbs steep ice and snow-covered rock bands; exposed and technical terrain requiring ice climbing skills; multiple pitches of WI2-WI3+ climbing likely
route_beta.snow_ice_conditions: June: best conditions, ice/snow-covered rock bands, compact ice; July+: deteriorating conditions, rockfall increase, loose rock exposed, warm ice unstable
route_beta.technical_details: Access via Wilson Glacier from southeast; climb steep headwall with rock bands and ice sections; requires ice climbing protection throughout; estimated multiple pitches; exact route-finding varies by conditions; considered one of extremely challenging and dangerous routes on Adams

hazards: Extremely high rockfall danger—primary hazard of route
Loose and weathered rock throughout headwall
Hydrothermal alteration creating unstable terrain
Steep ice and snow-covered rock with high exposure
Route mostly unrepeated due to hazards and difficulty
Thermal features and fumaroles at summit region
Rapid weather changes and rime ice formation
gear_required: Rock protection (small cams/nuts for mixed sections)

time_estimate.note: Estimated 3+ days; long pitches and complex route-finding; extreme caution required

routes.descent: Likely descent via Wilson Glacier and alternate route; many parties may use completely different descent line to minimize rockfall exposure
sources: USGS Twentieth Century Glacier Change on Mount Adams study
Climbing guide references noting unrepeated status

routes.name: Mount Formidable - South Route (Chasm Route)
routes.area: North Cascades National Park, Cascade Pass

watch_out.description: Route characterized as 'chossy mess' and 'formidable class 3 choss' with poor rock quality throughout. Climbers report 'very loose' rock in critical areas. Rock quality worsens toward summit.
watch_out.severity: High - contributes to fall risk throughout

watch_out.description: Notable exposed traverse involving a large chasm near summit requires protection. August 2025 report: 'chasm crossing was not trivial and should be treated with respect.' Party used rope with camming protection despite appearing straightforward. Exposed scrambling required.
watch_out.terrain: Steep, loose rock across exposed ledge

watch_out.description: Correct gully leading to summit is difficult to locate. Multiple trip reports note wrong gully entries leading to nearby peaklets instead of true summit. No consistent cairns or markers.
watch_out.risk: Wrong-turn exposure; climbers have settled for false summits

watch_out.description: Final approach involves steep, airy slabs and ledges with high exposure. Requires protection and solid scrambling skills.
watch_out.terrain: Steep rock faces with limited protection options

watch_out.description: Cache Glacier approach may have crevasses early in season. One geologist reported sketchy alcove above chasm difficult in early season.
watch_out.severity: Moderate to high depending on season

watch_out.description: Remote wilderness setting with no cell service and unlikely to encounter another party within a week. One fatality has occurred on this route. Heat exposure on return descent (August reports 'oppressive heat' through Box Canyon).
watch_out.mitigation: Tell someone your itinerary, carry emergency communication, plan for heat stress

watch_out.description: High alpine exposure at 8,325 ft. Variable mountain weather with potential afternoon storms. Alpine zone hazards: wind, lightning, temperature swings.
South route is only practical descent and involves the same difficult route-finding, constant exposure, and loose rock traversing downhill (higher fall risk).
watch_out.severity: Critical - same hazards as ascent, heightened by fatigue

routes.turnaround_strategy: Cache Col area (~7,300 ft) serves as psychological turnaround point. Recommend turning back if chasm crossing hasn't been reached by mid-afternoon due to descent hazard and light.
routes.recent_conditions: August 2025: Chasm crossing exposed but manageable with protection. June 2025: Up to 1 foot of snow possible.

routes.name: Mount Formidable - North Route via Ptarmigan Traverse
routes.area: North Cascades National Park, Cascade Pass
routes.approach: Via Cascade Pass trailhead, part of 35-mile Ptarmigan Traverse

watch_out.description: Known for exposure with Class 3 scrambling. Loose or steep snow may require short Class 4 scrambles to bypass poor snow conditions.
Mix-Up Arm traverse involves heather, talus, and snow travel. Early season presents loose or steep snow conditions.
watch_out.severity: Moderate - route-finding dependent on snow melt

watch_out.description: Full traverse crosses five glaciers with crevasse exposure and required glacier travel skills.
watch_out.mitigation: Rope team glacier travel skills essential

watch_out.description: High alpine ridge traverse exposed to rapid weather changes and afternoon thunderstorms.
Typical Cascades rock with loose sections throughout ridge traverse.

time_estimates.summit_day: Variable depending on full traverse vs out-and-back
time_estimates.descent: Same day or multi-day for full traverse

routes.turnaround_strategy: Flexible out-and-back allows turnaround at any point along Red Ledges or Mix-Up Arm based on conditions and party fitness.

routes.name: Eldorado Peak - East Ridge (Standard Route)
routes.area: North Cascades National Park, Inspiration Glacier

watch_out.description: Heavily crevassed glacier system below East Ridge. Late-season hazard: 'glacier is pretty much melted out and heavily crevassed.' Climbers must 'zickzack our way up and cross some pretty unstable looking snow bridges.' Early season (June): glide cracks forming on steep sections.
watch_out.locations: Inspiration Glacier approach and lower East Ridge
watch_out.severity: High - requires rope team and crevasse rescue skills

watch_out.description: Famous knife-edge summit ridge with up to 1,000+ feet of exposure on both sides. Ridge becomes extremely thin in late summer melt conditions. 'Knife's edge has one part with 1000+ ft exposure, so don't miss the boot path.' Crampons essential.
watch_out.terrain: Steep snow/ice on both sides; boot penetration and crampons provide critical security

watch_out.description: Crevasse crossings rely on snow bridges that deteriorate with temperature and sun exposure. Late-summer conditions make bridges 'less secure.' May 2025 reports: breakable crust and sloppy snow. Late April conditions: 30.5 feet snow accumulation.
watch_out.seasonal: July-August most hazardous; May-June better conditions
watch_out.mitigation: Night climbing recommended to exploit frozen conditions

watch_out.description: North Cascades weather highly variable. 'Clear mornings quickly turning into afternoon storms.' Rapid temperature swings affect rock/ice conditions and visibility. Smoke from nearby wildfires reported reducing visibility 180+ degrees.
watch_out.severity: Moderate to high - affects turnaround timing

watch_out.description: Final scramble to summit and descent portions contain loose rock. Temperature-dependent: 'by late summer crevasses may open and glacier travel becomes more complex. Everything is safer at night when temperatures are cold and the sun has not begun to melt the snow and ice, as the terrain is firmer for easier climbing... rock fall is less of a hazard.'
watch_out.temporal: Rockfall increases with sun exposure and warming

watch_out.description: 6,716 feet of gain in 2 days from trailhead to summit. Altitude hazard: 8,876 ft summit with rapid ascent increases risk of acute mountain sickness and compromised judgment.
watch_out.mitigation: High camp acclimatization important

watch_out.description: Descending in darkness requires careful navigation. Planted wands 'pretty useless at night.' Late-season trip starting at 5:15 PM required uncertain nighttime descent in new moon darkness.
watch_out.seasonal: Critical concern during summer solstice and warm-season alpine parties

watch_out.description: Initial approach crosses Cascade River which can be 'very difficult depending on the time of year' (melt-driven swelling).
watch_out.seasonal: July-August peak runoff; early June easier

time_estimates.summit_day: 5-7 hours climbing time from high camp
time_estimates.total: 1-3 days depending on parties; most fit climbers 1 day

routes.turnaround_strategy: Professional guides implement firm turnaround policies. 'Cannot guarantee that you will reach the summit due to weather, fitness, or route conditions.' Typical turnaround: if not at East Ridge crest by 9-10 AM, descend. Weather windows critical: turn back for afternoon storm development.
routes.required_skills: Glacier travel experience, rope team movement, self-arrest, basic crevasse rescue, ice axe and crampon proficiency
routes.season: Peak: June-September (May and late September marginal)
routes.recent_conditions: April 2026: 30.5 feet snow accumulation since September 2025, elevation 8,892.8 ft. June 2024: Mixed early summer below 6,000 ft, late winter above with sloppy conditions and glide cracks.

routes.name: Eldorado Peak - West Arete
routes.description: Perhaps the most spectacular alpine rock climb in the North Cascades. Rises nearly 3,000 feet from glacier to summit. Only climbed a few times per season by very competent parties.

watch_out.description: The route is 'generally pretty loose, dangerous, and stressful.' Early pitches feature 'unbelievably loose 5.6 terrain' and 'the loosest pitch either of us had ever climbed.' Upper pitches remain 'dangerously loose' despite reaching better-quality sections in the tower. Final 1,000 feet to summit involves loose rock.
watch_out.zones: Entire route; worse on lower and upper sections
watch_out.severity: Critical - protection reliability questionable; falling rock hazard significant

watch_out.description: Prominent tower section (crux area) requires navigation 'up, through or around the towers with relentless exposure on each side.' Passage involves multiple towers, 70-meter rappel, and complex route decisions. 'Old-school 5.8 face traverse' features only 'an in-situ piton' for protection while 'delicately' crossing with 'tremendous exposure.'
watch_out.terrain: Vertical rock faces, thin protection, high slab exposure
watch_out.severity: Critical - minimal protection, psychological pressure extreme

watch_out.description: Route-finding 'proved constant' throughout with pitches 'blending together' and no clear passages. Multiple tower sections require decision-making on ascent direction. Descending from upper sections 'would be a dangerous nightmare.'
watch_out.consequence: Once past towers, climbers are committed; retreat becomes near-impossible

watch_out.description: Rock quality unpredictable and variable throughout. Sections improve in tower area then degrade again toward summit.
watch_out.protection_impact: Reliable anchor placement difficult; protection expectations low

watch_out.description: Exposure on both sides of towers and thin ridge sections. 'Tremendous exposure' on face traverse sections. Ground-fall potential from most pitches.
watch_out.severity: Critical - falls likely to be fatal

watch_out.description: Extended multi-pitch climbing over 17+ hours from base camp (19 pitches documented). Physical and mental fatigue increase with climb length. Unplanned bivouacs common due to route complexity and climbing time.
watch_out.frequency: Significant percentage of ascents experience bivouac or retreat

watch_out.description: 3,000 feet of exposed rock climbing with minimal shelter. High alpine weather volatility affects afternoon stability and visibility.
Descent likely via other routes or complicated rappel sequences. Loose rock and navigation challenges on descent.

time_estimates.approach: Long and strenuous approach to base

routes.skill_requirements: Ability to climb 5.8 competently, ability to move quickly and efficiently on lower 5th class terrain, excellent ice axe and crampon skills, solid belaying and rappelling. Climbers should be in excellent physical condition.
routes.party_size: Parties should have 2-3 experienced rock climbers; larger parties move too slow
routes.hazard_summary: This is a serious, dangerous alpine rock climb with loose rock throughout, minimal protection opportunities, extreme exposure, and complex route-finding. Not for typical alpine climbers—requires rock climbing competence and commitment tolerance.

routes.name: Eldorado Peak - Northwest Ice Couloir
routes.description: Excellent mixed climb in scenic wild Marble Creek Cirque. Less common than East Ridge but offers superior ice climbing experience.

watch_out.description: Route 'guarded by a long, somewhat complicated approach' through Marble Creek Cirque. Navigation to couloir base required.
watch_out.mitigation: Require route beta from recent ascents

watch_out.description: Steep ice conditions requiring ice climbing skills. 'Fat ice' conditions in winter/early spring. January 2026 report: excellent soft fat ice conditions making for 'excellent climbing.'
Typical couloir rockfall hazard, particularly during warming or afternoon hours.
watch_out.mitigation: Early-morning start essential

watch_out.description: Exposed high alpine climbing with North Cascades weather volatility. Afternoon storms and rapid temperature changes.

Descent involves rappelling. Trip report notes: 'recommend bringing more ice screws' (had only 3 total) and 'a 60-meter rappel would be preferable to 40 meters for the descent off Inspiration.' Rappel anchor reliability critical.
watch_out.mitigation: Carry 4-6 ice screws, bring 60m rope for longer rappels

watch_out.description: 8,876 ft summit with rapid elevation gain introduces altitude hazard.

time_estimates.climbing: ~90 minutes from couloir base to summit

routes.required_skills: Ice climbing (AI2-3), ice axe/crampon proficiency, rappelling, route-finding, glacier navigation for approach
routes.equipment_notes: Bring 4-6 ice screws minimum. 60-meter rope recommended for descent rappels. Standard alpine rack for mixed sections.
routes.season: Winter-early spring (January-March) for optimal fat ice
routes.recent_conditions: February 2026: Excellent conditions with soft fat ice. Strong snowpack despite lean winter.

routes.name: Nooksack Tower - North Face (Beckey-Schmidtke Route)
routes.area: Mount Shuksan Massif, North Cascades, Washington

watch_out.timing: April-June peak, particularly afternoon thermal loading
watch_out.terrain: Couloir approach and descent gullies with 45-55 degree slopes
watch_out.frequency: High in spring, moderate early summer
watch_out.mitigation: Early morning starts (summit by noon), helmet mandatory, probe and assess snow stability

watch_out.terrain: North-facing arête and chimney pitches with lichen/moss coverage
watch_out.quality: Extremely loose and friable per historical first ascent notes
watch_out.rockfall_risk: Continuous from above and below during ascent and especially descent
watch_out.mitigation: Frequent anchor placement, test all holds carefully, expect slow climbing, constant helmet use

watch_out.terrain: Price Glacier approach heavily crevassed with hidden bridges, overhanging bergschrund at terminus
watch_out.exposure: Mid-late season (August-September) features 50-60 degree overhanging bergschrund requiring ice climbing technique
watch_out.consequence: Hidden crevasse fall hazard on approach, entrapment risk at bergschrund
watch_out.mitigation: Rope team traversal, systematic probing, ice climbing skills mandatory, rope rescue kit required

watch_out.terrain: Summit first rappel drops directly to Price Lake with 10+ rappels down 50+ degree couloir
watch_out.anchor_quality: Limited bomber anchor options in loose rock
watch_out.consequence: Fatal if anchor fails or rope gets stuck
watch_out.mitigation: Modern bomber anchor setup with multiple equalized points, autoblock backups on every rappel, test system before committing weight

watch_out.hazard: Severe Time Commitment and Benightment Risk
watch_out.duration: 10+ hours round-trip forces overnight bivvy planning or single-push endurance
watch_out.consequence: Technical couloir descent in darkness is extremely hazardous with rockfall and avalanche risk
watch_out.mitigation: Early season ascent to maximize daylight, bivvy gear mandatory, clear turnaround time (1-2 PM)

watch_out.pattern: Maritime climate brings Pacific moisture; afternoon thunderstorms common
watch_out.altitude_exposure: 8285 ft, above treeline with full weather exposure
watch_out.visibility: Can deteriorate rapidly with cloud formation

watch_out.landmarks: Multiple couloirs and potential off-route gullies with moss/lichen coverage
watch_out.error_consequence: Wrong gully choice leads to dead-ends and time loss
watch_out.complexity: Moderate-to-difficult in snow; easier on bare ground

watch_out.terrain: Steep couloir requires careful rope management and anchor placement
watch_out.difficulty: Greater than ascent in loose rock

routes.best_conditions: July-August (stable snow bridges, lowest avalanche, longest daylight)
routes.avoid: November-May (avalanche risk, short daylight, wet snow danger)
routes.turnaround: 1-2 PM mandatory to ensure daylight descent

routes.name: Mount Shuksan - Fisher Chimneys
routes.grade: Class 4, Grade IV-V (mixed rock and snow)

watch_out.terrain: Upper Curtis Glacier and approach slopes 40-50 degrees
watch_out.frequency: Debris fields indicate consistent spring and early summer slides
watch_out.factor: Rain significantly increases instability by adding weight and lubricating snowpack
watch_out.mitigation: Early season ascent, avoid after rain events, assess slope stability before climbing

watch_out.terrain: Upper Curtis Glacier beneath seracs, White Salmon Glacier area exposed to serac fall
watch_out.exposure: Active seracs hung above snow chutes and glacier passages
watch_out.consequence: Large falling blocks possible with minimal warning

watch_out.terrain: Chimney system Class 4 scrambling with frequent loose blocks
watch_out.guidebook: Fred Beckey describes as 'tortuous' route with poor rock quality
watch_out.hazard_level: Constant rockfall exposure from above and below
watch_out.characteristics: If moss/lichen-covered, you're off-route; clean rock from thousands of boots indicates correct line

watch_out.pattern: Route becomes increasingly dangerous as season progresses and glaciers break up
watch_out.exposure: Crevasses open and hidden bridges weaken
watch_out.consequence: Late-season attempts (Aug-Sept) have significantly higher risk

watch_out.terrain: Complex chimney system, steep exposed dirt trail with Class 4 scrambling
watch_out.difficulty: Class 4 corner sections are difficult with heavy pack
watch_out.landmarks: Crampon scratches on rock indicate correct path; follow trails of boot prints
watch_out.error_risk: Easy to wander into wrong gullies; route is 'tortuous'

watch_out.exposure: Full weather exposure on glaciers and upper couloirs
watch_out.visibility: Cloud formation rapid at high altitude

routes.best_conditions: June-July (stable snow bridges, lower avalanche risk)
routes.avoid: August-September (glacier breakup, increased rockfall, serac hazard)

routes.name: Mount Shuksan - North Face (Hanging Glacier Route)
routes.grade: Grade II-III, 45-50 degree steep snow/alpine ice

watch_out.terrain: Very active Hanging Glacier directly above approach and summit slopes
watch_out.consequence: Large avalanches can sweep White Salmon Creek drainage entirely
watch_out.frequency: Regular throughout season; largest deposits in spring
watch_out.debris: Massive debris fields indicate frequent large slides
watch_out.exposure: Direct path beneath active glacier slopes

watch_out.terrain: Large, unstable ice blocks overhanging approach slopes
watch_out.timing: Increases as summer heat destabilizes ice
watch_out.warning: Often falls without warning or with minimal acoustic indication

watch_out.terrain: Long snow couloir, may steepen to 50+ degrees near summit
watch_out.avalanche_risk: Steep slopes prone to slough avalanches throughout season

watch_out.terrain: Glacier crossing with hidden crevasses and snow bridges
watch_out.exposure: Dynamic crevasse field with bridges weakening throughout season

watch_out.complexity: Navigation on blank snow slopes can be disorienting in poor visibility
watch_out.risk: Easy to drift left/right into hanging glacier terrain

watch_out.pattern: Maritime storms develop rapidly, visibility can deteriorate quickly
watch_out.wind: Exposed ridge location subject to extreme winds

routes.best_conditions: Early July (minimal serac risk, stable snowpack)
routes.avoid: August+ (Hanging Glacier very active), May-June (avalanche risk)
routes.descent_strategy: White Salmon Glacier is common descent route (ties into standard routes)

watch_out.hazard: Serac Threat on Upper Curtis Glacier
watch_out.terrain: Seracs hung above snow chutes and climbing approach
watch_out.exposure: Direct line beneath overhanging ice formations

watch_out.terrain: Multiple 50-60 degree ice sections requiring ice climbing technique
Heavily crevassed glacier with dynamic field throughout season
watch_out.bridges: Snow bridges weaken significantly after July

watch_out.terrain: 40-50 degree approach slopes subject to sloughs

watch_out.complexity: Crevasse field navigation requires careful weaving
watch_out.visibility: Can be disorienting in poor visibility or whiteout

routes.name: Mount Shuksan - Northwest Arete
routes.grade: Grade III-IV, Class 4-5 (mixed rock and snow)

watch_out.hazard: Exposure to Northwest Couloir and Hanging Glacier
watch_out.terrain: Arete bordered by couloir on right (south) and Hanging Glacier terrain on left (north)
watch_out.consequence: Exposed climbing with glacier hazards on one side

watch_out.terrain: Nearby north-facing hanging glacier with active serac and avalanche hazard
Arete rock with exposure
watch_out.quality: Variable quality on North Cascades greenschist

watch_out.terrain: Mixed snow/rock especially in spring and early season

watch_out.exposure: Full weather exposure on exposed arete

first_ascent.significance: Took 3 years for second ascent (2010); very few ascents since

approach.terrain_type: PCT trail, then off-trail bushwhacking through alder and forest
approach.key_crossings: North Fork River log crossing, Grizzly Creek, extensive alder tunnel

route_description.climbing_method: Mostly simul-climbing on 3rd-4th class, belayed sections on harder terrain
route_description.rock_quality: Distinctly alpine throughout; excellent gneiss on harder pitches
route_description.crux_sections: Two pitches of 5.10 crack climbing on southeast peak headwall; 5.10 downclimb on overhanging pitch with 5,000 ft exposure below
route_description.notable_features: Dramatic ridge with exceptional sustained exposure; glacier-to-rock transition crossing; highly technical for alpine climb

gear.bivy_requirements: Summit bivy acceptable (tight for 2-3 people); sleeping bag rated to 30-40°F minimum

steps: Two 30m rappels to soft snow field
Skier's left exit; scramble crumbly ledges
Descend obvious snow couloir
Looker's right to avoid waterfall cliffs
5,000 ft cumulative descent; poor rock quality on exit

primary: Extreme exposure (5,000 ft sections)
Brittle rock - high rockfall risk especially on descent
Overhanging 5.10 downclimb pitch with massive exposure
Poor rock quality on rappel anchors
Difficult glacier-to-buttress moat crossing
Weather: afternoon thunderstorms, temperature swings
secondary: Remoteness: 3-year gap between first and second ascent
Endurance: 12+ hour climbing day after 11-12 hour approach

routes.ascent_count: Rare; only ~2-3 ascents beyond first ascent (as of 2021)

first_ascent.significance: Classic alpine route; most popular on the mountain

approach.terrain_type: PCT trail south, North Fork Bridge Creek trail northwest, then bushwhacking
creek_crossings: Multiple water crossings in forest section
approach.terrain_obstacles: Alder bushwhacking (1-2 hours of dense growth), increasing overgrowth on North Fork trail, Class 3 slabs right of waterfall

vertical_sections: Rope up for glacier travel (crampons required)
Two pitches low 5th class on red ledges
route_description.rock_quality: Poor to fair - exposed granitic slabs on lower section, bomber sections on crest
route_description.crux_pitches: Glacier-to-buttress transition via collapsed snow moat (Class 4 ledge traversal); left arrete (5.5 moves)

gear.bivy_requirements: Summit bivy (space for ~3 people); lightweight pad preferred; sleeping bag rated to 34°F minimum
gear.water: No reliable source on route - carry sufficient supply

descent.method: Rappels and scrambling via South Face
steps: 2 rappels to Black Tooth Notch (traverse-y, 30m pitches)
Class 4 ledge scramble to cairn (may require up/down scrambling)
4 additional rappels into southwest couloir (with loose rock hazard)
Class 2-3 terrain on gully descent
Open meadow and burn zone descent to Park Creek trail
descent.alternative_descent: South gully approach; avoid waterfall cliffs via looker's right

primary: Moat crossing: Variable difficulty, potentially impassable late season
Abundant loose rock: Rockfall hazard on rappels
Glacier crevasses
Overhead hazard: Seracs and icefall from receding glacier above
Extreme heat on exit in late summer (98°F+ in Stehekin area)
secondary: Poor rock quality requiring careful anchor placement
Limited bail-out options (loose terrain, no rap stations)

routes.popularity: Most popular route on mountain; on many climbers' wish lists

first_ascent.climbers: Wolf Bauer, Philip Dickett, Joe Halwax, Jack Hossack, George MacGowan
first_ascent.significance: Original route; still the least technical

options.name: Bridge Creek Approach (most common)
options.terrain_type: PCT trail, North Fork Bridge Creek trail, then off-trail to glacier
options.key_landmarks: Open area at base of Goode at trail end

options.name: Park Creek Approach (Stehekin)
options.notes: Road washouts above Highbridge make this approach less attractive

route_description.glacier_approach: Traverse west end of glacier to reach couloir
route_description.notable_features: Notch requiring downclimbing onto small glacier; steep gully with loose rock
route_description.crux_section: 300-foot headwall requiring testing each hold, intensified by ice pockets
route_description.rock_quality: Largely poor to terrible; loose rock throughout
route_description.snow_ice_conditions: Temperature-dependent; can have thin ice pockets; snow patches often last into August

specifics: Rock (minimal - for rappel anchors if needed)
Rope (30-60m, for safety anchor or rappel)
gear.rope_usage: Recommended for crevasse rescue on glacier approach

steps: Descend obvious snow couloir (direct route)
Class 3-4 scrambling in gully
Trail descent to base camp
descent.technical_difficulty: Low for descent; loose rock is primary hazard

primary: Loose and poor quality rock: Major rockfall hazard throughout
Glacier instability: Rumbling reported; seracs and crevasses
Icy conditions: Temperature-dependent thin ice on rock
Steep gully exposure
secondary: Moat crossing: Variable difficulty depending on melt stage
Weather: Afternoon thunderstorms, wind exposure at summit area

routes.best_season: Late July to August (snow patches stable into August)
routes.comparative_difficulty: Easiest technical grade, but poor rock quality makes it serious; rockfall hazard exceeds grade rating

routes.name: Alpental Ski Area Summer/Spring Route

trailhead.name: Alpental Base Lodge
trailhead.parking: Alpental parking lot; verify access post-road-closure

routes.descent: Edelweiss Bowl scree run or reverse approach

watch_out.description: Initial approach crosses Cascade River which can be 'very difficult depending on the time of year' (melt-driven swelling).
watch_out.seasonal: July-August peak runoff; early June easier

time_estimates.summit_day: 5-7 hours climbing time from high camp
time_estimates.total: 1-3 days depending on parties; most fit climbers 1 day

routes.turnaround_strategy: Professional guides implement firm turnaround policies. 'Cannot guarantee that you will reach the summit due to weather, fitness, or route conditions.' Typical turnaround: if not at East Ridge crest by 9-10 AM, descend. Weather windows critical: turn back for afternoon storm development.
routes.required_skills: Glacier travel experience, rope team movement, self-arrest, basic crevasse rescue, ice axe and crampon proficiency
routes.season: Peak: June-September (May and late September marginal)
routes.recent_conditions: April 2026: 30.5 feet snow accumulation since September 2025, elevation 8,892.8 ft. June 2024: Mixed early summer below 6,000 ft, late winter above with sloppy conditions and glide cracks.

routes.name: Eldorado Peak - West Arete
routes.description: Perhaps the most spectacular alpine rock climb in the North Cascades. Rises nearly 3,000 feet from glacier to summit. Only climbed a few times per season by very competent parties.

watch_out.description: The route is 'generally pretty loose, dangerous, and stressful.' Early pitches feature 'unbelievably loose 5.6 terrain' and 'the loosest pitch either of us had ever climbed.' Upper pitches remain 'dangerously loose' despite reaching better-quality sections in the tower. Final 1,000 feet to summit involves loose rock.
watch_out.zones: Entire route; worse on lower and upper sections
watch_out.severity: Critical - protection reliability questionable; falling rock hazard significant

watch_out.description: Prominent tower section (crux area) requires navigation 'up, through or around the towers with relentless exposure on each side.' Passage involves multiple towers, 70-meter rappel, and complex route decisions. 'Old-school 5.8 face traverse' features only 'an in-situ piton' for protection while 'delicately' crossing with 'tremendous exposure.'
watch_out.terrain: Vertical rock faces, thin protection, high slab exposure
watch_out.severity: Critical - minimal protection, psychological pressure extreme

watch_out.description: Route-finding 'proved constant' throughout with pitches 'blending together' and no clear passages. Multiple tower sections require decision-making on ascent direction. Descending from upper sections 'would be a dangerous nightmare.'
watch_out.consequence: Once past towers, climbers are committed; retreat becomes near-impossible

watch_out.description: Rock quality unpredictable and variable throughout. Sections improve in tower area then degrade again toward summit.
watch_out.protection_impact: Reliable anchor placement difficult; protection expectations low

watch_out.description: Exposure on both sides of towers and thin ridge sections. 'Tremendous exposure' on face traverse sections. Ground-fall potential from most pitches.
watch_out.severity: Critical - falls likely to be fatal

watch_out.description: Extended multi-pitch climbing over 17+ hours from base camp (19 pitches documented). Physical and mental fatigue increase with climb length. Unplanned bivouacs common due to route complexity and climbing time.
watch_out.frequency: Significant percentage of ascents experience bivouac or retreat

watch_out.description: 3,000 feet of exposed rock climbing with minimal shelter. High alpine weather volatility affects afternoon stability and visibility.
Descent likely via other routes or complicated rappel sequences. Loose rock and navigation challenges on descent.

time_estimates.approach: Long and strenuous approach to base

routes.skill_requirements: Ability to climb 5.8 competently, ability to move quickly and efficiently on lower 5th class terrain, excellent ice axe and crampon skills, solid belaying and rappelling. Climbers should be in excellent physical condition.
routes.party_size: Parties should have 2-3 experienced rock climbers; larger parties move too slow
routes.hazard_summary: This is a serious, dangerous alpine rock climb with loose rock throughout, minimal protection opportunities, extreme exposure, and complex route-finding. Not for typical alpine climbers—requires rock climbing competence and commitment tolerance.

routes.name: Eldorado Peak - Northwest Ice Couloir
routes.description: Excellent mixed climb in scenic wild Marble Creek Cirque. Less common than East Ridge but offers superior ice climbing experience.

watch_out.description: Route 'guarded by a long, somewhat complicated approach' through Marble Creek Cirque. Navigation to couloir base required.
watch_out.mitigation: Require route beta from recent ascents

watch_out.description: Steep ice conditions requiring ice climbing skills. 'Fat ice' conditions in winter/early spring. January 2026 report: excellent soft fat ice conditions making for 'excellent climbing.'
Typical couloir rockfall hazard, particularly during warming or afternoon hours.
watch_out.mitigation: Early-morning start essential

watch_out.description: Exposed high alpine climbing with North Cascades weather volatility. Afternoon storms and rapid temperature changes.

Descent involves rappelling. Trip report notes: 'recommend bringing more ice screws' (had only 3 total) and 'a 60-meter rappel would be preferable to 40 meters for the descent off Inspiration.' Rappel anchor reliability critical.
watch_out.mitigation: Carry 4-6 ice screws, bring 60m rope for longer rappels

watch_out.description: 8,876 ft summit with rapid elevation gain introduces altitude hazard.

time_estimates.climbing: ~90 minutes from couloir base to summit

routes.required_skills: Ice climbing (AI2-3), ice axe/crampon proficiency, rappelling, route-finding, glacier navigation for approach
routes.equipment_notes: Bring 4-6 ice screws minimum. 60-meter rope recommended for descent rappels. Standard alpine rack for mixed sections.
routes.season: Winter-early spring (January-March) for optimal fat ice
routes.recent_conditions: February 2026: Excellent conditions with soft fat ice. Strong snowpack despite lean winter.

route_beta.overall_grade: Grade II; Moderate Snow alpine climbing
route_beta.technical_assessment: One of Mount Baker's most remote routes; less technically difficult than Coleman Headwall or Cockscomb Ridge but demands strong glacier travel and crevasse rescue skills
glacier_approach_features: Five-mile approach staying high across valleys and small glaciers
Route follows Ptarmigan Ridge terrain with mixed snow/rock
Early season: difficult slog through heavy snow in steep chutes
Late season: melt-exposed route finding challenges
Best accessed by skiing from Heather Meadows parking lot (early season)

At ~9,000 ft: significant gaping crevasses requiring careful navigation

ascent_routes.name: Primary Route (Early Season)

ascent_routes.name: Alternate Route (Late Season/Technical Conditions)
ascent_routes.description: If early routes impassable, retreat to Park Glacier; climb headwall directly (AI3 ice climbing); requires several ice screw placements; continue snow/glacier ridge to summit

ice_snow_by_season.conditions: Good snow stability, manageable crevasse conditions, excellent corn snow lower elevations
Strong climbing conditions, crevasses begin opening with summer melt
Large gaping crevasses, significantly more hazardous, generally not recommended
