altitude_hazards.severity_level: Usually subsyndromic acute mountain sickness (mild); severe altitude illness rare
altitude_hazards.acclimatization: Overnight camp at 8,000-9,500 ft helps; hydration critical
altitude_hazards.fatigue: Cumulative effect on late-season Roman Wall difficulty

thermal_stress.glacier_reflection: Intensifies UV; sunburn common even on cloudy days
thermal_stress.dehydration: Meltwater streams unreliable; carry water; electrolyte management critical at altitude

afternoon_cascade.description: Mid-day warming → meltwater destabilizes snow bridges → crevasses open → rockfall increases
afternoon_cascade.bergschrund_effect: Becomes impassable by end of day
afternoon_cascade.mitigation: Start before dawn; summit by early afternoon; descend before midday warming cycle

fatigue_cascade.description: Roman Wall comes after 4-6 hours of sustained climbing
fatigue_cascade.descent_risk: Increased risk on descent (psychological challenge on narrow boot packs)
fatigue_cascade.mitigation: Strong acclimatization; overnight camp; physical training; pacing discipline

ice_axes.mandatory_feature: Adze blade (for anchor creation and self-arrest)
ice_axes.pick_design: Straight or slightly curved for alpine ice

crampons.front_points: Horizontal dual configuration (superior for variable snow/ice)
crampons.binding: Semi-automatic (strap + lever at heel) easiest/most efficient
crampons.requirement: Boot must have toe AND heel welt
crampons.anti_balling_plates: ABS plastic plates mandatory for late-season glassy terrain

rope.party_optimization: 3-4 people recommended (distributes rescue responsibility; enables mechanical advantage systems)
rope.caveat: 2-person teams high-skill only (single rescuer must arrest + execute rescue simultaneously)

ice_screws.10cm: NOT EN 568 certified; marginal/high-risk; should NOT be relied upon
ice_screws.13cm: CE certified but too short for primary crevasse rescue anchors
ice_screws.16cm_minimum: Recommended for Easton Glacier (~10 kN holding strength)
ice_screws.19_21cm: Optimal for soft ice and V-thread creation

mechanical_advantage: 5:1 or 6:1 practical necessity (required for unassisted body recovery in soft ice)
anchor_systems: T-slot horizontal deadman: 30-50cm depth, 4-8 kN strength (snow anchors)

protection_personal.helmet: EN 12492 certified (Black Diamond Half Dome, Petzl Sirocco)
protection_personal.helmet_standards: Impact testing: 5kg striker from 2m, force limit ≤10 kN; Note: EN 12492 does NOT regulate SPF
protection_personal.belay_device: ATC Alpine Guide (8-10mm rated) or GriGri2

personal_supplies.water: 2-3 liters (melt streams unreliable; hydration critical at 10,000 ft)
personal_supplies.sunscreen: SPF 50+ minimum (glacier reflection + altitude intensifies UV)
personal_supplies.lip_balm: SPF 30+ (chapped lips common hazard at altitude)
personal_supplies.insulation: Layer system (overnight camp requires 0°F-rated sleeping bag if staying high)

summit_day_from_high_camp_8500_9000_ft.phase: High camp to glacier base (~8,500 ft)

summit_day_from_high_camp_8500_9000_ft.notes: Technical crux; fatigue factor; psychological challenge
Depends on fitness, conditions, party size

full_trip_option_1_overnight_camp.day_1: Trailhead → high camp (~8,500 ft) — 4-6 hours hiking + setup
full_trip_option_1_overnight_camp.day_2: High camp → summit → high camp — 6-9.5 hours climbing + descent
full_trip_option_1_overnight_camp.total: 2-3 day trip (most parties extend to 3 days for rest/acclimatization)

full_trip_option_2_single_push.risk: Fatigue on Roman Wall; no margin for delays/crevasse rescue
full_trip_option_2_single_push.recommendation: Not recommended for first-timers or parties with weak glacier skills

critical_timing.reason: Avoid afternoon warming, which destabilizes snow bridges + increases rockfall
critical_timing.practice: Pre-dawn departure from high camp (typically 4-5 AM)

critical_timing.overnight_benefit: Provides some acclimatization; additional rest day recommended if arriving from sea level

descent_duration.roman_wall_descent: 30-60 min (psychologically more challenging; narrow boot packs)
descent_duration.note: Descent typically faster than ascent (30-40% faster for experienced parties)

time_sensitive_decision_points.decision: If later, may need to end-run (late-season)
If later, assess light + descent capability
Ideal summit time; weather window evaluation

upper_slopes_to_bergschrund.navigation: Follow boot pack downslope; careful footing on descending teams
upper_slopes_to_bergschrund.glissade_option: May use glissade if snow/ice conditions allow

bergschrund_crossing_reverse.technique: End-run traverse in reverse (left-to-right, descending 40° terrain)

upper_slopes_to_bergschrund.caution: Weakened snow bridges in midday sun; afternoon melt may make crossing more difficult than ascent

roman_wall_descent.ice_quality: Polished, slippery (worse than ascent for boot traction)
roman_wall_descent.technique: Controlled descent on steep angle; downhill glissade risky if crevasse field below
roman_wall_descent.narrow_boot_packs: Psychological exposure; slow progress (1-2 min per person crossing crux zone)
roman_wall_descent.rope_configuration: Continuous belay or rappel (depending on conditions/party preference)
roman_wall_descent.exposure: Rockfall risk remains; afternoon warming increases rock dislodgement

mid_glacier_descent.glissade_opportunity: Late-season slushy snow may allow glissade sections (faster descent)

lower_glacier_descent.glissade: Common on firm snow (early season) or slush (late season)

boulder_field_to_trailhead.terrain: Loose rock descent; careful footing (twisted ankle common)
boulder_field_to_trailhead.hydration: Drink remaining water; streams reappear on lower trail

descent_summary_by_section.technical: Most technically challenging; narrow boot packs
Fast if glissade, slow if firm

alternative_descent_options.name: Direct Descent (Not Recommended)
alternative_descent_options.description: Glissade from mid-glacier to lower glacier (fastest if snow conditions perfect)
alternative_descent_options.risk: Loss of control on steep/icy sections; uncontrolled descent into hidden crevasses
alternative_descent_options.recommendation: Only attempted by experienced parties in optimal conditions

alternative_descent_options.name: Rappel Descent (Commercial Guides)
alternative_descent_options.description: Some guide services rappel Roman Wall crux (faster, more controlled)
alternative_descent_options.requirements: Fixed anchors, rope setup, rescue capability

alternative_descent_options.name: Delayed Descent (Emergency/Weather)
alternative_descent_options.description: If bad weather/avalanche risk develops, may need to bivouac at high camp
alternative_descent_options.shelter: Use emergency bivy sack; overnight care critical (hypothermia risk)

high_confidence: Grade classifications (Grade III / Grade II Alpine; AI1-AI2)
Roman Wall location, angle (40-45°), and status as primary crux
Bergschrund techniques (early-season snow bridge vs. late-season end-running)
Time estimates (5-8 hours summit day typical)
SR-542 seasonal closure patterns (winter closure Nov-May, variable spring opening)
moderate_confidence: Exact ice angles on Roman Wall in late season (60°+ compression)
Mechanical advantage ratios (5:1 practical standard vs. 3:1 teaching standard)
Specific trip report times (varies by fitness, conditions, party size)
lower_confidence: Exact avalanche risk statistics (difficult to quantify)
Road closure precise dates (daily variability; check WSDOT before driving)
Specific condition reports older than 2 years (glacier/snow changes annually)

quick_reference.technical_difficulty: 3/5; requires solid cramponing + ice axe skills
quick_reference.elevation: 10,781 ft summit; 7,600 ft gain from trailhead
quick_reference.primary_crux: Roman Wall (40-45° base, up to 60° late season); 1,000 ft
quick_reference.summit_day_duration: 5-8 hours climbing from high camp (~8,500 ft)
quick_reference.primary_hazards: Crevasses, bergschrund (late-season), Roman Wall rockfall, altitude
quick_reference.early_season_advantage: Snow bridges; straightforward access; firm conditions
quick_reference.late_season_challenge: Glassy ice; bergschrund opening; higher technical difficulty
quick_reference.sr_542_access: Mid-June to mid-October typical; check WSDOT daily

routes.name: East Ridge (Degenhardt Route)
routes.first_ascent: Unknown; named after peak first ascensionist William Degenhardt

pitch_breakdown.terrain: Mixed class 4–5, traverse to south face
South face traverse and wrap-around to final pitches

rock_quality.hazards: Significant loose rock and rockfall risk throughout

protection.density: Minimal on most pitches except first; single rack of nuts sufficient on upper pitches
protection.strategy: Place protection on first pitch; minimal pro on subsequent pitches (choss-fest)

gear.runners: Bring plenty of double runners (5+) and slings for choss
gear.protection: Nuts, small–medium cams preferred over larger gear
gear.safety: Helmet (required), rock shoes or approach shoes

approach_to_route.terrain: Steep descent into Crescent Creek Basin (mixed snow/talus, 400 ft wet slabby 3rd/4th class climbing), ~1-hour rising traverse across basin
approach_to_route.route_finding: Marked by cairns but can be tricky; exposed terrain

time_estimate.total_from_camp: 8–10 hours round-trip (depending on conditions and team speed)
time_estimate.notes: High variability due to loose rock and routefinding challenges

hazards: Extremely loose rock and rockfall (primary hazard)
Crumbly rock quality reduces security
Exposure on ridge; significant air
Weather exposure; weather windows critical
Routefinding challenges in upper terrain
Wet slabby terrain on approach (3rd/4th class)--slipping risk
routes.weather_exposure: Very exposed; afternoon thunderstorm risk; high altitude weather changes rapidly

descent.anchor_type: Downclimbing, some rappel anchors if needed
descent.total_time: Included in 8–10 hour estimate from camp

routes.user_notes: Route is less popular due to choss factor and loose rock hazard. Rock quality is arguably worse than reputation suggests. Expect to manage protection carefully and avoid dislodging large amounts of debris on partners below.

routes.name: North Face / North Buttress (Stoddard Route)
routes.first_ascent_original: 1961 by Charles Bell, Ed Cooper, David Hiser, Mike Swayne (III 5.7, line of least resistance to right of crest)
routes.first_ascent_stoddard: 1984 by John Stoddard (bold solo onsight ascent of cleaner, knife-edge ridge crest; 5.8+)
routes.popularity: Classic, committing line; fewer ascents than West Ridge due to difficulty and commitment
routes.route_description: Impressive north side rises dramatically 2,500 feet from McMillan Cirque to summit. Route ascends directly from base to summit via prominent buttress.

pitch_breakdown.protection: Protection possible but not always necessary

pitch_breakdown.terrain: Low-angle 5th class rock (can be simul-climbed as single mega-pitch or broken into pitches)
pitch_breakdown.protection: Moderate pro placement; some runners useful

pitch_breakdown.section: Upper buttress (2,000+ ft to summit block)
pitch_breakdown.terrain: Knife-edge ridge of blocky rock (Stoddard route); steeper, more technical
pitch_breakdown.protection: Crux pitches require good protection; blocky rock quality variable

rock_quality.mid_sections: Generally solid on crest; varied off-crest
rock_quality.upper_sections: Clean, solid rock on main crest (Stoddard line)
rock_quality.summit_approach: Slightly looser blocky rock near false summit and west peak
rock_quality.overall_assessment: Much better than East Ridge; Stoddard line features inspiring, cleaner rock

protection.type: Trad (cams, nuts) and natural anchors (slings, rappel stations)
protection.strategy: Medium alpine rack emphasized; cams preferred to nuts on upper pitches
protection.lower_sections: Can simul-climb with minimal pro; protection available if desired
protection.upper_pitches: Better protection; crux sections require good placements

gear.rack: Medium alpine rack with emphasis on medium cams and slings
gear.runners: Multiple slings and alpine runners for belay anchors
gear.ice_protection: Ice axe and crampons necessary for glacier traversal and snow
gear.ice_screws: 1–2 ice screws for glacier moat protection if needed
gear.belay: Sling and bolt anchors on upper route; rappel stations marked

approach_to_route.from_crescent_creek_basin: 2–3 hours even for fast parties to base of route at McMillan Cirque
approach_to_route.terrain: Traverse Crescent Creek Basin (scree/talus with hidden snow and ice ramps); ascend to col between Himmelhorn and Ottohorn
approach_to_route.glacier_crossing: Descent from col via downclimbing steep snow/ice or rappelling to McMillan Cirque; traverse across glaciated terrain to base of North Buttress
approach_to_route.moat: Negotiate glacier moat to reach climbing base; ice screw may be helpful

time_estimate.climb_only: 5–7 hours (rough estimate; can be simul-climbed in sections to save time)
time_estimate.descent: Not separately documented; typically 2–3 hours via West Ridge descent
time_estimate.total_trip: 3–4 days total (includes 1–2 day approach, climb day, exit day)

hazards: Objective hazard: rockfall and ice from above (2,500 feet of elevation; exposure to entire north side)
Loose blocky rock in upper sections (though better than East Ridge)
Long, exposed climbing (committing); weather window critical
Glacier hazards: moats, crevasses (may be belayed), avalanche exposure (generally low risk in summer)
Complex routefinding in upper sections (especially in poor visibility)
Exposure to afternoon weather changes at altitude
routes.weather_exposure: Highly exposed; weather windows critical for safety

descent.method: West Ridge descent (easy and uneventful on ridge)
descent.hazards: Loose rock on off-route sections; significant off-route bushwhacking on return adds ~6 hours
descent.anchor_type: Natural anchors and slings; no fixed rappels required if staying on ridge
descent.notes: Return bushwhacking to base camp is time-consuming; allow extra hours for route-finding exit

routes.climbing_style_notes: Original 1961 route follows line of least resistance (easier angle, right of crest). Stoddard 1984 route follows cleaner, more aesthetic knife-edge crest (higher grade, better rock, more inspiring but more exposed).

routes.name: Southeast Buttress
routes.rock_grade_note: Conservative/older sources list 5.9; current grade is 5.10a (confirmed by 2 separate 2025 ascents)
routes.rock_type: Granite - splitter cracks with hand cracks, finger cracks, wide cracks, chimneys
routes.approach_detail: 18+ miles from trailhead to Upper Cathedral Lake (via Boundary Trail, Chewuch River, or Andrews Creek approaches); 30 minutes from camp to climb base
routes.climb_detail: Approximately 7 hours base to summit; can vary with pitch linking and experience level
routes.descent_detail: 45 minutes back to camp; descend ridge west from summit, traverse into gully, cross back over ridge, descend west/south on loose slopes to trail
routes.total_time: ~7-8 hours base to summit + 5+ hours approach

hazards.exposure: High - multiple exposed sections, 'infamous jump across' required on descent (either handholds or jump technique)
hazards.rockfall_loose_rock: Moderate - noted in helmets-recommended guidance; one 2016 ascent noted 'little loose rock' despite warnings
hazards.route_finding: Low - clear route, minimal navigation issues reported
hazards.descent_technical: High - exposed downclimb/traverse mandatory; no-fall terrain on descent
hazards.belay_quality: Good mostly; some semi-hanging belays noted

protection.recommendation: Doubles of #0.2-2.0 Camalot, single #3, offset nuts; #4 cam occasionally useful

weather_patterns.june_conditions: Fair amount of snow still present
weather_patterns.july: Fire season risk (early-mid July typical closure)
weather_patterns.aug_sept: Ideal - thunderstorm risk afternoon storms common but manageable
weather_patterns.october: Bluebird conditions often reported, cold nights after mid-Sept

routes.seasonal_variation: June snowmelt variable year-to-year; fire season (July) main access constraint; early October often clearest
routes.bailout_points: Can descend from Lower Cathedral Lake approach if conditions deteriorate; descent route allows retreat to scree slopes
watch_out: Infamous 'jump across' on descent - mandatory, requires confidence with exposure
Loose rock on approach/descent slopes - helmet recommended
Route requires trad rack - no bolts

confidence.time: MEDIUM (7 hours from one detailed 2016 report)

routes.recent_ascent: August 31, 2025 - Nick Sweeney & Avi Baehr (3.5 hours climb time with pitch linking)

sources.title: Cathedral Peak Southeast Buttress Trip Report

routes.name: South Face
routes.approach_detail: 18+ miles from trailhead via one of three approach options
routes.climb_detail: Estimated 7+ hours (similar scale to SE Buttress)

hazards.rockfall: Moderate-High - large face exposure increases rockfall risk
hazards.weather: Significant - high altitude face exposure to storms

routes.first_ascent: Fred Beckey, Dave Wagner, John Brottem, Doug Leen - 1968 (rated F8 A1 in original notation)

routes.name: Southwest Slope (Easiest Route)
routes.commitment_level: Moderate - day climb viable from camp
routes.approach_detail: 18+ miles to Upper Cathedral Lake via Andrews Creek Trail or other approaches
routes.climb_detail: Approximately 1.5 hours round trip from Upper Cathedral Lake camp
routes.route_description: Follow climbers trail from Upper Cathedral Lake; Class 2-3 scrambling on granite blocks and slabs; 'step-across' ledge feature requiring exposure management near summit

hazards.technical: Moderate - 'infamous leap of faith crack' requires either handholds to pull across or jump technique on descent; this is the crux technical obstacle

seasonal_variation.june: Fair amount of snow in lower sections, snowmelt variable
seasonal_variation.july: Fire season risk (early-mid July typical closure)
seasonal_variation.october: Excellent - clear skies common, but cold nights after mid-Sept

watch_out: 'Leap of faith crack' on descent - mandatory move requiring either handholds or jump across
Afternoon thunderstorm risk - start early from trailhead
routes.best_season: Mid-July through early October (post-fire season, post-snowmelt)

sources.title: Cathedral Lakes Loop Trip Report - Trail Cat Jim

routes.name: Nooksack Tower - North Face (Beckey-Schmidtke Route)
routes.area: Mount Shuksan Massif, North Cascades, Washington

watch_out.timing: April-June peak, particularly afternoon thermal loading
watch_out.terrain: Couloir approach and descent gullies with 45-55 degree slopes
watch_out.frequency: High in spring, moderate early summer
watch_out.mitigation: Early morning starts (summit by noon), helmet mandatory, probe and assess snow stability

watch_out.terrain: North-facing arête and chimney pitches with lichen/moss coverage
watch_out.quality: Extremely loose and friable per historical first ascent notes
watch_out.rockfall_risk: Continuous from above and below during ascent and especially descent
watch_out.mitigation: Frequent anchor placement, test all holds carefully, expect slow climbing, constant helmet use

watch_out.terrain: Price Glacier approach heavily crevassed with hidden bridges, overhanging bergschrund at terminus
watch_out.exposure: Mid-late season (August-September) features 50-60 degree overhanging bergschrund requiring ice climbing technique
watch_out.consequence: Hidden crevasse fall hazard on approach, entrapment risk at bergschrund
watch_out.mitigation: Rope team traversal, systematic probing, ice climbing skills mandatory, rope rescue kit required

watch_out.terrain: Summit first rappel drops directly to Price Lake with 10+ rappels down 50+ degree couloir
watch_out.anchor_quality: Limited bomber anchor options in loose rock
watch_out.consequence: Fatal if anchor fails or rope gets stuck
watch_out.mitigation: Modern bomber anchor setup with multiple equalized points, autoblock backups on every rappel, test system before committing weight

watch_out.hazard: Severe Time Commitment and Benightment Risk
watch_out.duration: 10+ hours round-trip forces overnight bivvy planning or single-push endurance
watch_out.consequence: Technical couloir descent in darkness is extremely hazardous with rockfall and avalanche risk
watch_out.mitigation: Early season ascent to maximize daylight, bivvy gear mandatory, clear turnaround time (1-2 PM)

watch_out.pattern: Maritime climate brings Pacific moisture; afternoon thunderstorms common
watch_out.altitude_exposure: 8285 ft, above treeline with full weather exposure
watch_out.visibility: Can deteriorate rapidly with cloud formation

watch_out.landmarks: Multiple couloirs and potential off-route gullies with moss/lichen coverage
watch_out.error_consequence: Wrong gully choice leads to dead-ends and time loss
watch_out.complexity: Moderate-to-difficult in snow; easier on bare ground

watch_out.terrain: Steep couloir requires careful rope management and anchor placement
watch_out.difficulty: Greater than ascent in loose rock

routes.best_conditions: July-August (stable snow bridges, lowest avalanche, longest daylight)
routes.avoid: November-May (avalanche risk, short daylight, wet snow danger)
routes.turnaround: 1-2 PM mandatory to ensure daylight descent

routes.name: Mount Shuksan - Fisher Chimneys
routes.grade: Class 4, Grade IV-V (mixed rock and snow)

watch_out.terrain: Upper Curtis Glacier and approach slopes 40-50 degrees
watch_out.frequency: Debris fields indicate consistent spring and early summer slides
watch_out.factor: Rain significantly increases instability by adding weight and lubricating snowpack
watch_out.mitigation: Early season ascent, avoid after rain events, assess slope stability before climbing

watch_out.terrain: Upper Curtis Glacier beneath seracs, White Salmon Glacier area exposed to serac fall
watch_out.exposure: Active seracs hung above snow chutes and glacier passages
watch_out.consequence: Large falling blocks possible with minimal warning

watch_out.terrain: Chimney system Class 4 scrambling with frequent loose blocks
watch_out.guidebook: Fred Beckey describes as 'tortuous' route with poor rock quality
watch_out.hazard_level: Constant rockfall exposure from above and below
watch_out.characteristics: If moss/lichen-covered, you're off-route; clean rock from thousands of boots indicates correct line

watch_out.pattern: Route becomes increasingly dangerous as season progresses and glaciers break up
watch_out.exposure: Crevasses open and hidden bridges weaken
watch_out.consequence: Late-season attempts (Aug-Sept) have significantly higher risk

watch_out.terrain: Complex chimney system, steep exposed dirt trail with Class 4 scrambling
watch_out.difficulty: Class 4 corner sections are difficult with heavy pack
watch_out.landmarks: Crampon scratches on rock indicate correct path; follow trails of boot prints
watch_out.error_risk: Easy to wander into wrong gullies; route is 'tortuous'

watch_out.exposure: Full weather exposure on glaciers and upper couloirs
watch_out.visibility: Cloud formation rapid at high altitude

routes.best_conditions: June-July (stable snow bridges, lower avalanche risk)
routes.avoid: August-September (glacier breakup, increased rockfall, serac hazard)

routes.name: Mount Shuksan - North Face (Hanging Glacier Route)
routes.grade: Grade II-III, 45-50 degree steep snow/alpine ice

watch_out.terrain: Very active Hanging Glacier directly above approach and summit slopes
watch_out.consequence: Large avalanches can sweep White Salmon Creek drainage entirely
watch_out.frequency: Regular throughout season; largest deposits in spring
watch_out.debris: Massive debris fields indicate frequent large slides
watch_out.exposure: Direct path beneath active glacier slopes

watch_out.terrain: Large, unstable ice blocks overhanging approach slopes
watch_out.timing: Increases as summer heat destabilizes ice
watch_out.warning: Often falls without warning or with minimal acoustic indication

watch_out.terrain: Long snow couloir, may steepen to 50+ degrees near summit
watch_out.avalanche_risk: Steep slopes prone to slough avalanches throughout season

watch_out.terrain: Glacier crossing with hidden crevasses and snow bridges
watch_out.exposure: Dynamic crevasse field with bridges weakening throughout season

watch_out.complexity: Navigation on blank snow slopes can be disorienting in poor visibility
watch_out.risk: Easy to drift left/right into hanging glacier terrain

watch_out.pattern: Maritime storms develop rapidly, visibility can deteriorate quickly
watch_out.wind: Exposed ridge location subject to extreme winds

routes.best_conditions: Early July (minimal serac risk, stable snowpack)
routes.avoid: August+ (Hanging Glacier very active), May-June (avalanche risk)
routes.descent_strategy: White Salmon Glacier is common descent route (ties into standard routes)

watch_out.hazard: Serac Threat on Upper Curtis Glacier
watch_out.terrain: Seracs hung above snow chutes and climbing approach
watch_out.exposure: Direct line beneath overhanging ice formations

watch_out.terrain: Multiple 50-60 degree ice sections requiring ice climbing technique
Heavily crevassed glacier with dynamic field throughout season
watch_out.bridges: Snow bridges weaken significantly after July

watch_out.terrain: 40-50 degree approach slopes subject to sloughs

watch_out.complexity: Crevasse field navigation requires careful weaving
watch_out.visibility: Can be disorienting in poor visibility or whiteout

routes.name: Mount Shuksan - Northwest Arete
routes.grade: Grade III-IV, Class 4-5 (mixed rock and snow)

watch_out.hazard: Exposure to Northwest Couloir and Hanging Glacier
watch_out.terrain: Arete bordered by couloir on right (south) and Hanging Glacier terrain on left (north)
watch_out.consequence: Exposed climbing with glacier hazards on one side

watch_out.terrain: Nearby north-facing hanging glacier with active serac and avalanche hazard
Arete rock with exposure
watch_out.quality: Variable quality on North Cascades greenschist

watch_out.terrain: Mixed snow/rock especially in spring and early season

watch_out.exposure: Full weather exposure on exposed arete

approach.trailhead: Dosewallips Road at Highway 101, Brinnon (11 miles south of Quilcene), milepost 306

roadApproach.description: Dosewallips Road, closed to vehicles at 2002 washout

lakeConstanceTrail.terrain: Class 2-3 scrambling, steep with roots and ledges, exposed cliff bands in final 0.5 miles

lakeToTraverse.terrain: Talus, scree, snow/ice seasonal, Class 3-4 scrambling

routeBeta.description: Downsloping ledge with finger jams in horizontal hand crack above cliff; crosses a green slab with good handholds at top, limited footholds initially
routeBeta.crux: Narrow ledge around corner with fingerjam crack; limited foothold availability in first 50 feet
routeBeta.rockQuality: Pillow basalt (lava); crumbly in places, decent holds on traverse slab
routeBeta.technicalDetails: Class 3 ledge (Class 3+ to 4 with snow/ice); straightforward when dry; single fall would be fatal; 50-100 foot traverse across slab with funnel drainage below cliff
routeBeta.rappelAnchors: Fixed pitons present, can set solo-belay anchors with pitons
routeBeta.ropeUsage: Some parties rope-up for protection despite Class 3 rating; brings 30-50 feet rope primarily for snow/ice conditions

primary: Fatal exposure above cliff (single fall consequence)
Loose scree throughout (August: one step up, two steps down)
Loose/crumbly pillow basalt rock
Limited foothold areas
Route-finding complexity in Avalanche Canyon
weatherRelated: Whiteouts and rapid weather changes at high elevation
Weather exposure on airy summit ridge
Snow/ice instability (soft snow over ice in early summer, hard corn snow late June-July)
seasonalHazards: May-June: Avalanche risk in Avalanche Canyon approach, snow-covered route

essential: Ice axe (essential for snow/ice; used for self-arrest and belaying)
Rope: 30-50 feet (for protection on traverse in snow/ice; group rappels/self-belay)
Boots: Mountain boots with good ankle support; scrambling requires rock-quality grip
optional: Climbing shoes (for green slab sections; most use approach boots)
gearRequirements.ropeAnchors: Pickets, buried rope, or rock horns (limited solid rock)

day2_summit.note: Extreme party: 8 hours up + 11 hours down

timeEstimates.seasonalVariation: Early season (May-June) slower due to snow; late season (August) faster on rock but slowed by scree

descentDescription.primaryRoute: South Chute via Finger Traverse (descend opposite of ascent)
descentSteps: From summit, traverse south-southeast to South Ridge low point/saddle
Access Finger Traverse at approximately 7,435 feet (airy exposed ledge above cliff)
When snow present, place pickets for self-belay or group rappel
Traverse ledge carefully with ice axe in one hand
Exit onto open slopes southeast of summit
Descend broad South Chute (scree gully) 1,200 feet through loose scree and talus
May rope down steep snow sections; otherwise scramble/slide scree to 6,600 ft ridgeline
Continue down Avalanche Canyon talus/scree back to Lake Constance
descentDescription.alternateRoute: Bypass gully circumvents lower finger traverse section; descend directly through bypass
descentDescription.rappelling: 30-50 feet anchored rappels possible on steep snow sections with T-anchors from pickets or buried rope; most parties avoid rappelling due to loose rock hazard and tangled ropes
descentDescription.returnTocamp: Exit Avalanche Canyon via talus/scree (0.6 miles), return to lake camp 3.5-4.5 hours from summit
descentDescription.exitToVehicle: Lake Constance to trailhead 3-4 hours (downhill, steep), Dosewallips Road 2-3 hours, total descent camp-to-vehicle 5-7 hours

routes.partySize: 2-4 typical; solo possible but exposure increases risk significantly

dataVerification.gps: Confirmed via PeakVisor, WTA, climbing databases
dataVerification.elevation: 7,743 ft (most common) vs 7,756 ft (PeakVisor); <15 ft variance

gradeAndClass.description: Multi-pitch alpine rock climbing with exposure

approach.trailhead: Dosewallips Road at Highway 101, Brinnon (11 miles south of Quilcene)
approach.criticalNoteRoadClosure: Dosewallips Road closed to vehicles since 2002 washout at ~4 miles; modern approach requires biking washout (~4 miles each way) or hiking it (5+ hours)

approach.approach_type: Multi-day trip essential (3-day weekend typical: approach day, climb day, descent day)
approach.terrain: Abandoned road, Lake Constance trail (steep, Class 2-3 scrambling), talus/scree fields to start of climb

routeBeta.description: Multi-pitch alpine rock route on west-facing arête; crumbly pillow basalt throughout
routeBeta.crux: Upper wall involves 5.4 groove climbing with existing pitons; followed by hand traverse on often snow-covered slab; concludes in open-book chimney
routeBeta.rockQuality: NOTORIOUS for loose pillow basalt; entire route features crumbly rock; protection relies more on slings and natural anchors than traditional wedges
routeBeta.simulClimbing: 5+ hour climbing day typical with simul-climbing possible
routeBeta.rappelAnchors: Fixed pitons present (condition questionable); natural anchors
routeBeta.altitude: 1,100 vertical feet of climbing above Crystal Pass

primary: Loose pillow basalt rock throughout route (exceptional hazard)
Exposure and airy sections
Fixed pitons in questionable condition
Poor descent visibility (especially in fog)
Avalanche risk in Avalanche Canyon approach
Significant route-finding difficulty on descent
descent: Descent can extend to 11+ hours in poor conditions
No organized rappel route off arête itself
Multiple descent options (Finger Traverse most common—very airy; Terrible Traverse more serious)
seasonal: May-June: Snow covers route (requires crampons/ice axe)
July-August: Best climbing conditions, but massive loose scree on descent

gearRequirements.rockRack: Full rock climbing rack (assumes crampons and natural anchors primary; traditional wedges less effective)
gearRequirements.note: Full alpine climbing kit with ice climbing capability essential for early season

threeDayExpedition.day1: Drive + approach to base camp/Crystal Pass area
threeDayExpedition.day2: Summit day (5-6 hours climbing, 3-4 hours descent minimum)

timeEstimates.seasonalVariation: Early season may extend due to snow; late season faster on rock but slowed by scree

descentDescription.primaryRoute: One of several options (Finger Traverse most common, very airy horizontal hand crack traverse; Terrible Traverse more serious)
descentDescription.routeFinding: Notorious for confusion in fog; descent can extend to 11+ hours in poor conditions
descentDescription.hazardNote: Route-finding difficulty is a MAJOR descent hazard on Mount Constance

approach.trailhead: Dosewallips Road, Highway 101 near Brinnon (11 miles south of Quilcene), turn at milepost 306

approach.permits: Backcountry wilderness permit (self-issued, no fee); Recreation.gov registration recommended

routeBeta.description: Not technical rock climbing pitches; purely Class 3-4 scramble terrain
routeBeta.type: Alpine scramble route with multiple variations

terribleTraverseSpecifics.ledgeType: Narrow exposed ledge with funnel-like drainage below leading to 500-foot vertical cliff
terribleTraverseSpecifics.exposure: HIGH - single fall would be fatal
terribleTraverseSpecifics.crux: Narrow ledge traversal with funnel drainage below cliff edge
terribleTraverseSpecifics.rockQuality: Pillow basalt (lava); generally crumbly, loose scree throughout; exposed areas have decent holds

routeBeta.southChuteCharacteristics: Broad scree gully system with loose talus descent (1,200 feet vertical)
routeBeta.summitApproach: Navigating through gaps, ledges, Class 3 chimney on 60-foot summit block
routeBeta.ropeAnchors: Fixed pickets or belay anchors NOT standard on this route; when rope deployed, pickets placed at firm snow above cliff edges; alternative: buried rope or rock features (limited solid rock)

fatalHazards: FATAL EXPOSURE: Terrible Traverse crosses above 500-foot cliff with funnel drainage below; single fall is fatal
rockHazards: Loose scree (August and late season: 'one step up, two steps down' nightmare)
navigationHazards: Route-finding complexity (Avalanche Canyon navigation notoriously difficult)
'Are you lost?' is common sentiment among climbers
The Thumb landmark marks canyon entrance
weatherHazards: Rapid weather changes at high elevation
Weather exposure on airy summit ridge
seasonalSnowHazards: May-June avalanche risk in Avalanche Canyon (assessment required)
Soft snow over ice in early summer
Transitions to hard corn snow in late June-July
otherHazards: Scrambling with high exposure (ridge traversing to final summit)
Steep scrambling on final summit stack

essential: Crampons (highly recommended early season, May-July; sharp spikes needed for hard snow/ice)
Ice axe (essential for snow/ice; used for self-arrest and belaying)
Rope: 30-50 feet (brings for protection on Terrible Traverse in snow/ice conditions; group rappels/self-belay)
Pickets: 3-5 (belaying on firm steep snow above cliff; NOT rock protection)
Helmet (essential due to loose rock and exposure risk)
Boots: Mountain boots with good ankle support; scrambling requires rock climbing-quality grip
optional: Climbing shoes (some parties for scrambling sections)
Trekking poles (strongly recommended for scree/talus approach)
packRequirements: 30-40L day pack for summit day (lighter is better on steep terrain)
gearRequirements.rockRack: NOT needed (not technical rock climbing route; pickets for snow anchors only)

day2_summit.extremeOutlier: 8 hours up + 11 hours down = 19 hours (not typical)

timeEstimates.seasonalVariation: Early season (May-June) slower due to snow; late season (August) faster on rock but slowed by massive scree

descentDescription.primaryDescentRoute: South Chute via Terrible Traverse (opposite of North Chute ascent)
descentSteps: From summit, traverse south-southeast to South Ridge low point/saddle
Access Terrible Traverse at ~7,435 feet elevation
Encounter narrow exposed ledge above 500-foot cliff funnel
When snow present and firm/steep, parties place pickets for self-belay or group rappel
Traverse ledge carefully with ice axe in one hand; climbing with other
Exit onto open slopes southeast of summit after traversing
Descend broad South Chute (scree gully) with 1,200 feet of talus/loose scree
May rope down sections if snow-filled, otherwise scramble/slide scree
Continue down Avalanche Canyon talus/scree back to Lake Constance
descentDescription.alternateDescentRoute: Finger Traverse / Bypass gully (circumvents lower sections via bypass route; descend directly through bypass to canyon floor)

rappellingNotes.deployment: If rope deployed, typically 30-50 feet of anchored rappels on steep snow sections only
rappellingNotes.anchors: Pickets (T-anchors), buried rope, or limited rock features
rappellingNotes.hazard: Low-angle terrain rappelling with loose rock is hazardous and tedious; ropes tangle easily
rappellingNotes.typical: Most parties avoid rappelling and downclimb/scramble instead

seasonRecommendation.july: Best alpine conditions; massive loose scree on descent

routes.partySize: 2-4 typical; solo ascents possible but exposed sections significantly increase risk

routeClassification.scrambleClass: Class 3-4 (non-technical rock, but exposed with serious consequences)

routes.description: The route ascends the N/NW face, generally starting at the glacier's right side and proceeding straight up while sticking to the right 1/3 of the glacier. Climbers encounter primarily moderate snow with some steep snow and a bit of alpine ice, with potential sections requiring ice screws and belays. Most terrain allows simul-soloing with picket protection.
hazards: Crevasses
Very loose volcanic rock on N. Ridge descent
Potential rockslides on other parties

routes.description: A moderate alpine snow route that avoids the crowds often found on the Easton or Coleman Demming. The climb follows the Boulder-Park Cleaver across relatively crevasse-free terrain with multiple summit finish options involving moderate snow slopes and optional rock climbing via 4th class direct line or north edge snow/ice slope.
hazards: Rockfall hazard while traversing below the 9,300ft buttress
Rockfall hazard when taking direct line north of the buttress
Steep cliffs just below Grant Peak
routes.season_best_months: Summer (terrain becomes very broken by mid-summer)

routes.grade: AI2-3 M4 Steep Snow (alpine/mixed), Easy 5th class
routes.description: After crossing beneath the Nooksack Tower at about 6,500 ft, step onto the Price Glacier and navigate steep snow, alpine ice at grades around AI2-3. The route ascends to bergschrunds around 7,800 ft with multiple line options (rock to left, ice line up middle, mixed to right), then continues to the summit pyramid via easy gullies (4th or low 5th class) on the south side.
hazards: Seracs
Slough chutes
Overhead seracs near bergschrund
routes.aspect: South-facing summit approach; north/northwestern glacial terrain

routes.grade: 5.4 YDS / 4a French / IV UIAA / VD British / AI1-2 Steep Snow
routes.description: A complex alpine climbing route on Nooksack Tower spanning approximately 1,000 feet with 10 pitches of varied climbing combining rock, ice, snow, and steep snow terrain.

routes.description: A moderate glacier climb frequently used as a descent route. The ascent involves navigating the White Salmon Glacier around crevasses to Winnie's Slide (6,800 ft), then crossing to Curtis Glacier via Hell's Highway (a snow chute between rock cliffs), before reaching the summit pyramid via the West Ridge or central gully.
hazards: Active Hanging Glacier with large avalanche potential
Rockfall hazard at Hell's Highway
Crevasse difficulties later in season
Party-induced rockfall on central gully route

routes.description: A complex alpine route on Mt. Shuksan's Main Peak featuring a steep and exposed chimney system that traverses multiple glaciers including White Salmon, Upper Curtis, and Sulphide Glaciers, ending at the summit pyramid. Route-finding requires careful attention to detail through the chimney system on the Shuksan Arm.
hazards: Exposed scrambling
Crevasse danger on glaciers
Moats
Steep snow/ice sections
Difficult descent requiring rappelling
gear_needed: Ice screws (potentially needed if Winnie's Slide is icy)
Second tool (potentially needed in late season)

routes.aspect: North and west-facing; highly exposed sections
